  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe used for column lookup.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size in inches.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Line color.

  • linewidth (float) – Line width.

  • linestyle (str) – Line style.

  • marker (Optional[str]) – Marker style.

  • markersize (int) – Marker size.

  • alpha (float) – Line opacity from 0 to 1.

  • grid (bool) – Whether to show grid lines.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Grid opacity.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • font_size (int) – Tick and legend font size.

  • title_size (int) – Title font size.

  • label_size (int) – Axis-label font size.

  • dpi (int) – Figure DPI when a new figure is created.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • ax (Optional[MatplotlibAxes]) – Existing axes to draw into.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior: "drop", "raise", or "keep".

  • sort_x (bool) – Whether to sort paired observations by x before drawing.

  • rolling_window (Optional[int]) – Optional rolling mean window for y values.

  • rolling_color (str) – Rolling mean line color.

  • fill_to (Optional[float]) – Optional baseline for filled area under the line.

  • fill_alpha (float) – Area fill opacity.

  • hline (Optional[float]) – Optional horizontal reference line.

  • vline (Optional[float]) – Optional vertical reference line.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.plot.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the line plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved or plotted.

  • ValueError – If input lengths differ, inputs are empty, or options are invalid.

Example

`python ax = dv.line_plot_static("date", "sales", data=df, rolling_window=7) `

Notes

sort_x=True is helpful when observations are not already ordered.

dataviz.line_plot_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, marker_color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2.0, mode: str = 'lines', marker_size: int = 8, showlegend: bool = True, hovermode: str = 'x unified', template: str = 'plotly', font_size: int = 12, title_size: int = 16, height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop', sort_x: bool = False, rolling_window: int | None = None, rolling_color: str = 'crimson', fill_to: float | None = None, hline: float | None = None, vline: float | None = None, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive line plot that shows ordered or time-based changes.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or a column name when data is provided.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or a column name when data is provided.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe used for column lookup.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Line color.

  • marker_color (Optional[str]) – Alias for color.

  • linewidth (float) – Line width.

  • mode (str) – Plotly scatter mode.

  • marker_size (int) – Marker size.

  • showlegend (bool) – Whether to show the legend.

  • hovermode (str) – Plotly hover mode.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • font_size (int) – Base font size.

  • title_size (int) – Title font size.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior: "drop", "raise", or "keep".

  • sort_x (bool) – Whether to sort paired observations by x before drawing.

  • rolling_window (Optional[int]) – Optional rolling mean window for y values.

  • rolling_color (str) – Rolling mean line color.

  • fill_to (Optional[float]) – Optional baseline for filled area under the line.

  • hline (Optional[float]) – Optional horizontal reference line.

  • vline (Optional[float]) – Optional vertical reference line.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Configured Plotly line figure.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved or plotted.

  • ValueError – If input lengths differ, inputs are empty, or options are invalid.

Example

`python fig = dv.line_plot_interactive("date", "sales", data=df, sort_x=True) `

Notes

Area fill is implemented with Plotly’s tozeroy mode when fill_to is zero.

dataviz.lollipop_chart(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Category', ylabel: str = 'Count', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, stem_color: str = 'gray', top_n: int | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static lollipop chart for category counts.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Dot color.

  • stem_color (str) – Stem line color.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of categories to keep.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the lollipop chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = lollipop_chart_static(categories) `

Notes

Lollipop charts reduce visual weight compared with full-width bars.

dataviz.lollipop_chart_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Category', ylabel: str = 'Count', color: str | None = None, top_n: int | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive lollipop chart for category counts.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input categorical observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Dot and stem color.

  • top_n (Optional[int]) – Optional number of categories to keep.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive lollipop chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no categories remain or top_n is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = lollipop_chart_interactive(categories) `

Notes

The line trace draws stems and the marker trace draws the lollipop heads.

dataviz.lorenz_curve(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'gray', theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static Lorenz curve.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input non-negative observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Curve color.

  • reference_color (str) – Equality reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the Lorenz curve.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If values contain negatives or sum to zero.

Examples

`python ax = lorenz_curve_static(values) `

Notes

The farther the curve lies below equality, the higher the concentration.

dataviz.lorenz_curve_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'gray', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 700, width: int = 700) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive Lorenz curve.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input non-negative observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Curve color.

  • reference_color (str) – Equality reference line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive Lorenz curve.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If values contain negatives or sum to zero.

Examples

`python fig = lorenz_curve_interactive(values) `

Notes

The equality reference line represents perfectly even distribution.

dataviz.lorenz_curve_values(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Tuple[ndarray, ndarray][source]

Compute Lorenz curve coordinates for non-negative values.

Parameters:

data (SeriesLike) – Input non-negative observations.

Returns:

Cumulative population share and cumulative value share.

Return type:

tuple[numpy.ndarray, numpy.ndarray]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If values contain negatives or sum to zero.

Examples

`python population, value_share = lorenz_curve_values(values) `

Notes

The curve starts at (0, 0) and ends at (1, 1).

dataviz.mad_outliers(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, threshold: float = 3.5) → Series[source]

Flag observations with large robust MAD z-scores.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • threshold (float) – Absolute MAD z-score threshold.

Returns:

Boolean mask where True marks a robust outlier.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If threshold is not positive or no observations remain.

Examples

`python mask = mad_outliers([1, 2, 3, 100]) `

Notes

MAD outliers are useful when the mean and standard deviation are distorted by extremes.

dataviz.mad_zscores(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, scale: float = 1.4826) → Series[source]

Compute robust z-scores using the median absolute deviation.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • scale (float) – MAD scaling constant.

Returns:

Robust z-scores aligned to cleaned numeric observations.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If scale is not positive or no observations remain.

Examples

`python scores = mad_zscores([1, 2, 3, 100]) `

Notes

A zero MAD returns zeros because robust spread cannot be estimated.

dataviz.missing_data_plot(df: DataFrame, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (12, 6), color: str | None = None, edgecolor: str = 'black', linewidth: float = 1.0, alpha: float = 0.7, grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static missing-value summary by dataframe column.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • df (pd.DataFrame) – Input dataframe containing the variables to visualize.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size as (width, height) in inches. Defaults to (12, 6).

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • edgecolor (str) – Configuration value for edgecolor. Defaults to 'black'.

  • linewidth (float) – Configuration value for linewidth. Defaults to 1.0.

  • alpha (float) – Configuration value for alpha. Defaults to 0.7.

  • grid (bool) – Configuration value for grid. Defaults to True.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Configuration value for grid_alpha. Defaults to 0.3.

  • theme (str) – Named styling theme applied after the base plot is created. Defaults to 'default'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 10.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 14.

  • label_size (int) – Configuration value for label_size. Defaults to 11.

  • dpi (int) – Configuration value for dpi. Defaults to 100.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context used while building the figure. Defaults to 'default'.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the rendered static chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Example

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.missing_data_plot_static(df) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.missing_data_plot_interactive(df: DataFrame, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, marker_color: str | None = None, showlegend: bool = True, hovermode: str = 'closest', template: str = 'plotly', font_size: int = 12, title_size: int = 16, height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive missing-value summary by dataframe column.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • df (pd.DataFrame) – Input dataframe containing the variables to visualize.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • marker_color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for marker_color. Defaults to None.

  • showlegend (bool) – Configuration value for showlegend. Defaults to True.

  • hovermode (str) – Configuration value for hovermode. Defaults to 'closest'.

  • template (str) – Plotly template used to style the interactive figure. Defaults to 'plotly'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 12.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 16.

  • height (int) – Plotly figure height in pixels. Defaults to 600.

  • width (int) – Plotly figure width in pixels. Defaults to 1000.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured Plotly figure containing the rendered interactive chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Example

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.missing_data_plot_interactive(df) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.moving_range_chart(data: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, span: int = 2, title: str = 'Moving Range Chart', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (12, 6), color: str = 'orange', theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static moving range chart.

Parameters:

  • data (ArrayLike) – Process observations.

  • span (int) – Difference span used for moving ranges.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • color (str) – Moving range line color.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

Returns:

Moving range chart axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If data cannot be converted to numeric observations.

  • ValueError – If span is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = dv.spc.moving_range_chart_static(values) `

Notes

Moving ranges are useful alongside individuals charts.

dataviz.moving_range_chart_interactive(data: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, span: int = 2, title: str = 'Moving Range Chart', color: str = 'orange', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 500, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive moving range chart.

Parameters:

  • data (ArrayLike) – Process observations.

  • span (int) – Difference span used for moving ranges.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • color (str) – Moving range line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive moving range chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If data cannot be converted to numeric observations.

  • ValueError – If span is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = dv.spc.moving_range_chart_interactive(values) `

Notes

Control limits are computed from the moving ranges themselves.

dataviz.normality_test(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, method: Literal['shapiro', 'normaltest'] = 'shapiro', alpha: float = 0.05) → NormalityTestResult[source]

Run a normality test for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • method (NormalityMethod) – Test method, either "shapiro" or "normaltest".

  • alpha (float) – Significance threshold used for the normality decision.

Returns:

Statistic, p-value, method, and decision.

Return type:

NormalityTestResult

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If method or alpha is invalid, or too few observations are available.

Examples

`python result = normality_test(data, method="shapiro") `

Notes

Shapiro-Wilk requires at least three observations; D’Agostino-Pearson requires at least eight.

dataviz.np_chart(defects: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, sample_size: int, title: str = 'np Chart', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (12, 6), theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static np chart for defective counts with constant sample size.

Parameters:

  • defects (ArrayLike) – Defective counts per sample.

  • sample_size (int) – Constant sample size.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

Returns:

np chart axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If defects cannot be converted to numeric values.

  • ValueError – If counts or sample size are invalid.

Examples

`python ax = dv.spc.np_chart_static(defects, sample_size=100) `

Notes

Use a p chart when subgroup sample sizes vary.

dataviz.np_chart_interactive(defects: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, sample_size: int, title: str = 'np Chart', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 500, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive np chart for defective counts with constant sample size.

Parameters:

  • defects (ArrayLike) – Defective counts per sample.

  • sample_size (int) – Constant sample size.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive np chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If defects cannot be converted to numeric values.

  • ValueError – If counts or sample size are invalid.

Examples

`python fig = dv.spc.np_chart_interactive(defects, sample_size=100) `

Notes

The center line represents the expected defective count.

dataviz.numeric_or_none(values: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray) → Series | None[source]

Try to coerce values to numeric data.

Parameters:

values (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

Returns:

Numeric values when coercion leaves at least one observation, otherwise None.

Return type:

Optional[pandas.Series]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If no meaningful conversion can be attempted.

Examples

`python numeric = numeric_or_none(series) `

Notes

This helper is permissive and is intended for profile functions, not strict plotting APIs.

dataviz.ordered_category_counts(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, order: Sequence[str] | None = None, normalize: bool = False, dropna: bool = True) → Series[source]

Compute category counts in a meaningful order.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input ordinal or categorical observations.

  • order (Optional[Sequence[str]]) – Explicit category order.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to return proportions instead of counts.

  • dropna (bool) – Whether to omit missing values.

Returns:

Ordered category counts or proportions.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the requested order omits observed categories.

Examples

`python counts = ordered_category_counts(responses, order=["Low", "Medium", "High"]) `

Notes

Without order, categories are sorted by their natural label order.

dataviz.ordinal_bar(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, order: Sequence[str] | None = None, normalize: bool = False, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static ordered category bar chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input ordinal or categorical observations.

  • order (Optional[Sequence[str]]) – Explicit category order.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to plot proportions instead of counts.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Bar color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the ordered bar chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the requested order omits observed categories.

Examples

`python ax = ordinal_bar_static(ratings, order=["Low", "Medium", "High"]) `

Notes

Ordered bars avoid misleading frequency sorting for ordinal variables.

dataviz.ordinal_bar_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, order: Sequence[str] | None = None, normalize: bool = False, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive ordered category bar chart.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input ordinal or categorical observations.

  • order (Optional[Sequence[str]]) – Explicit category order.

  • normalize (bool) – Whether to plot proportions instead of counts.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Bar color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive ordered bar chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the requested order omits observed categories.

Examples

`python fig = ordinal_bar_interactive(ratings) `

Notes

The function keeps the caller-supplied order on the x-axis.

dataviz.outlier_mask(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5) → Series[source]

Compute an outlier mask using a named rule.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule: "iqr", "zscore", or "mad".

  • threshold (float) – Threshold for z-score or MAD rules.

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier for Tukey fences.

Returns:

Boolean mask where True marks outliers.

Return type:

pandas.Series

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If the rule or thresholds are invalid.

Examples

`python mask = outlier_mask(values, rule="mad") `

Notes

This helper provides a shared rule interface for cap, remove, and flag workflows.

dataviz.outlier_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, method: Literal['iqr', 'zscore'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Observation', ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, outlier_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static index plot that highlights univariate outliers.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • method (OutlierMethod) – Outlier rule, either "iqr" or "zscore".

  • threshold (float) – Z-score threshold when method="zscore".

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier when method="iqr".

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Regular point color.

  • outlier_color (str) – Highlight color for outlier points.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Axes containing the outlier diagnostic plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If the selected outlier rule or threshold is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = outlier_plot_static(data, method="iqr") `

Notes

The x-axis is the cleaned observation order after missing values are removed.

dataviz.outlier_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, method: Literal['iqr', 'zscore'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Observation', ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, outlier_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive index plot that highlights univariate outliers.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • method (OutlierMethod) – Outlier rule, either "iqr" or "zscore".

  • threshold (float) – Z-score threshold when method="zscore".

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier when method="iqr".

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Regular point color.

  • outlier_color (str) – Highlight color for outlier points.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive outlier diagnostic plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If the selected outlier rule or threshold is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = outlier_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

Hover labels make it easy to inspect extreme observations by index.

dataviz.outlier_scatter(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, method: Literal['zscore', 'iqr'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 1.5, title: str = 'Outlier Scatter', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), normal_color: str = 'steelblue', outlier_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes

Create a static scatter plot highlighting bivariate outliers.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • method (Literal["zscore", "iqr"]) – Outlier detection method.

  • threshold (float) – Z-score threshold or IQR multiplier.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • normal_color (str) – Color for non-outlier observations.

  • outlier_color (str) – Color for outlier observations.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Scatter plot axes with outliers highlighted.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If threshold or method is invalid.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.outlier_scatter_static(x, y, method="zscore", threshold=3) `

Notes

A point is highlighted when either its x or y value is considered an outlier.

dataviz.outlier_scatter_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, method: Literal['zscore', 'iqr'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 1.5, title: str = 'Outlier Scatter', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Figure[source]

Create an interactive scatter plot highlighting bivariate outliers.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • method (Literal["zscore", "iqr"]) – Outlier detection method.

  • threshold (float) – Z-score threshold or IQR multiplier.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Interactive scatter plot with outliers highlighted.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If threshold or method is invalid.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.outlier_scatter_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

Outlier labels are computed from x and y independently.

dataviz.outlier_treatment_comparison(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', treatment: Literal['cap', 'remove'] = 'cap', title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static before-after box plot for outlier treatment.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule used for treatment.

  • treatment (Literal["cap", "remove"]) – Treatment method.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Box color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing before-after treatment comparison.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If treatment or rule options are invalid.

Examples

`python ax = outlier_treatment_comparison_static(values) `

Notes

This plot is intended for treatment review, not automatic data cleaning approval.

dataviz.outlier_treatment_comparison_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', treatment: Literal['cap', 'remove'] = 'cap', title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive before-after box plot for outlier treatment.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule used for treatment.

  • treatment (Literal["cap", "remove"]) – Treatment method.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Box color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive before-after treatment comparison.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If treatment or rule options are invalid.

Examples

`python fig = outlier_treatment_comparison_interactive(values) `

Notes

Hover labels expose original and treated distributions side by side.

dataviz.p_chart(defects: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, sample_sizes: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, title: str = 'p Chart', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (12, 6), theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static p chart for defect proportions.

Parameters:

  • defects (ArrayLike) – Defective counts per sample.

  • sample_sizes (ArrayLike) – Sample sizes for each count.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

Returns:

p chart axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be converted to numeric values.

  • ValueError – If counts and sample sizes are incompatible.

Examples

`python ax = dv.spc.p_chart_static(defects, sample_sizes) `

Notes

Limits are based on average sample size when subgroup sizes vary.

dataviz.p_chart_interactive(defects: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, sample_sizes: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, title: str = 'p Chart', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 500, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive p chart for defect proportions.

Parameters:

  • defects (ArrayLike) – Defective counts per sample.

  • sample_sizes (ArrayLike) – Sample sizes for each count.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive p chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be converted to numeric values.

  • ValueError – If counts and sample sizes are incompatible.

Examples

`python fig = dv.spc.p_chart_interactive(defects, sample_sizes) `

Notes

The chart displays defective proportions rather than raw counts.

dataviz.pareto_chart(categories: Sequence[str], counts: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, title: str = 'Pareto Chart', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (12, 6), theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static Pareto chart for defect categories.

Parameters:

  • categories (Sequence[str]) – Defect category labels.

  • counts (ArrayLike) – Defect counts for each category.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

Returns:

Pareto chart primary axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If counts cannot be converted to numeric values.

  • ValueError – If categories and counts lengths differ.

Examples

`python ax = dv.spc.pareto_chart_static(["Scratch", "Dent"], [12, 5]) `

Notes

Bars are sorted descending and the secondary axis shows cumulative percent.

dataviz.pareto_chart_interactive(categories: Sequence[str], counts: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, title: str = 'Pareto Chart', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive Pareto chart for defect categories.

Parameters:

  • categories (Sequence[str]) – Defect category labels.

  • counts (ArrayLike) – Defect counts for each category.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive Pareto chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If counts cannot be converted to numeric values.

  • ValueError – If categories and counts lengths differ.

Examples

`python fig = dv.spc.pareto_chart_interactive(categories, counts) `

Notes

Cumulative percent is plotted against the right y-axis.

dataviz.percentile_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, step: int = 5, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Percentile', ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, marker: str = 'o', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static percentile profile plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • step (int) – Percentile interval between points.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional line color.

  • marker (str) – Marker style.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.plot.

Returns:

Axes containing the percentile profile.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If step is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = percentile_plot_static(data, step=10) `

Notes

Percentile profiles provide a compact view of distribution shape.

dataviz.percentile_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, step: int = 5, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str = 'Percentile', ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive percentile profile plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • step (int) – Percentile interval between points.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive percentile profile.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If step is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = percentile_plot_interactive(data, step=10) `

Notes

Percentile values are available in hover labels.

dataviz.percentile_table(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, step: int = 10) → DataFrame[source]

Create a percentile table for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • step (int) – Percentile interval between rows.

Returns:

Percentile and value columns.

Return type:

pandas.DataFrame

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If step is not between 1 and 100 or no observations remain.

Examples

`python table = percentile_table(data, step=5) `

Notes

The table always includes 0 and 100 percentiles.

dataviz.pp_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static probability-probability plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name used as the theoretical reference.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional marker color.

  • reference_color (str) – Reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Axes containing the PP plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or the distribution name is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = pp_plot_static(data) `

Notes

PP plots compare cumulative probabilities rather than quantiles.

dataviz.pp_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 700, width: int = 700, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive probability-probability plot.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name used as the theoretical reference.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional marker color.

  • reference_color (str) – Reference line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive PP plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or the distribution name is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = pp_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

The 45-degree reference line represents perfect distributional agreement.

dataviz.precision_recall_curve(precision: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, recall: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, ap: float | None = None, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2.0, linestyle: str = '-', grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static precision-recall curve for classification performance.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • precision (ArrayLike) – Precision values for the precision-recall curve.

  • recall (ArrayLike) – Recall values for the precision-recall curve.

  • ap (Optional[float]) – Average precision value to display in the legend. Defaults to None.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size as (width, height) in inches. Defaults to (10, 6).

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • linewidth (float) – Configuration value for linewidth. Defaults to 2.0.

  • linestyle (str) – Configuration value for linestyle. Defaults to '-'.

  • grid (bool) – Configuration value for grid. Defaults to True.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Configuration value for grid_alpha. Defaults to 0.3.

  • theme (str) – Named styling theme applied after the base plot is created. Defaults to 'default'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 10.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 14.

  • label_size (int) – Configuration value for label_size. Defaults to 11.

  • dpi (int) – Configuration value for dpi. Defaults to 100.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context used while building the figure. Defaults to 'default'.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the rendered static chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Example

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.precision_recall_curve_static(precision, recall) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.precision_recall_curve_interactive(precision: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, recall: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, ap: float | None = None, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, marker_color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2.0, showlegend: bool = True, hovermode: str = 'closest', template: str = 'plotly', font_size: int = 12, title_size: int = 16, height: int = 600, width: int = 700, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive precision-recall curve for classification performance.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • precision (ArrayLike) – Precision values for the precision-recall curve.

  • recall (ArrayLike) – Recall values for the precision-recall curve.

  • ap (Optional[float]) – Average precision value to display in the legend. Defaults to None.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • marker_color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for marker_color. Defaults to None.

  • linewidth (float) – Configuration value for linewidth. Defaults to 2.0.

  • showlegend (bool) – Configuration value for showlegend. Defaults to True.

  • hovermode (str) – Configuration value for hovermode. Defaults to 'closest'.

  • template (str) – Plotly template used to style the interactive figure. Defaults to 'plotly'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 12.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 16.

  • height (int) – Plotly figure height in pixels. Defaults to 600.

  • width (int) – Plotly figure width in pixels. Defaults to 700.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured Plotly figure containing the rendered interactive chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Example

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.precision_recall_curve_interactive(precision, recall) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.prediction_plot(y_true: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, y_pred: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, marker_size: int = 50, alpha: float = 0.6, edgecolor: str = 'black', linewidth: float = 1.0, line_color: str = 'red', line_style: str = '--', line_width: float = 2.0, grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static comparison of observed and predicted regression values.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • y_true (ArrayLike) – Observed target values.

  • y_pred (ArrayLike) – Predicted target values.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size as (width, height) in inches. Defaults to (10, 6).

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • marker_size (int) – Configuration value for marker_size. Defaults to 50.

  • alpha (float) – Configuration value for alpha. Defaults to 0.6.

  • edgecolor (str) – Configuration value for edgecolor. Defaults to 'black'.

  • linewidth (float) – Configuration value for linewidth. Defaults to 1.0.

  • line_color (str) – Configuration value for line_color. Defaults to 'red'.

  • line_style (str) – Configuration value for line_style. Defaults to '--'.

  • line_width (float) – Configuration value for line_width. Defaults to 2.0.

  • grid (bool) – Configuration value for grid. Defaults to True.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Configuration value for grid_alpha. Defaults to 0.3.

  • theme (str) – Named styling theme applied after the base plot is created. Defaults to 'default'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 10.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 14.

  • label_size (int) – Configuration value for label_size. Defaults to 11.

  • dpi (int) – Configuration value for dpi. Defaults to 100.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context used while building the figure. Defaults to 'default'.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the rendered static chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Example

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.prediction_plot_static(y_true, y_pred) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.prediction_plot_interactive(y_true: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, y_pred: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, marker_color: str | None = None, marker_size: int = 8, line_color: str = 'red', line_dash: str = 'dash', showlegend: bool = True, hovermode: str = 'closest', template: str = 'plotly', font_size: int = 12, title_size: int = 16, height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive comparison of observed and predicted regression values.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • y_true (ArrayLike) – Observed target values.

  • y_pred (ArrayLike) – Predicted target values.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • marker_color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for marker_color. Defaults to None.

  • marker_size (int) – Configuration value for marker_size. Defaults to 8.

  • line_color (str) – Configuration value for line_color. Defaults to 'red'.

  • line_dash (str) – Configuration value for line_dash. Defaults to 'dash'.

  • showlegend (bool) – Configuration value for showlegend. Defaults to True.

  • hovermode (str) – Configuration value for hovermode. Defaults to 'closest'.

  • template (str) – Plotly template used to style the interactive figure. Defaults to 'plotly'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 12.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 16.

  • height (int) – Plotly figure height in pixels. Defaults to 600.

  • width (int) – Plotly figure width in pixels. Defaults to 1000.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured Plotly figure containing the rendered interactive chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Example

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.prediction_plot_interactive(y_true, y_pred) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.process_distribution(data: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, bins: int = 30, title: str = 'Process Distribution', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (12, 6), theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static process distribution histogram with sigma bands.

Parameters:

  • data (ArrayLike) – Process observations.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

Returns:

Process distribution axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If data cannot be converted to numeric observations.

  • ValueError – If bins is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = dv.spc.process_distribution_static(values) `

Notes

Vertical lines mark mean and plus/minus one, two, and three sigma.

dataviz.process_distribution_interactive(data: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, bins: int = 30, title: str = 'Process Distribution', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive process distribution histogram with sigma bands.

Parameters:

  • data (ArrayLike) – Process observations.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive process distribution chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If data cannot be converted to numeric observations.

  • ValueError – If bins is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = dv.spc.process_distribution_interactive(values) `

Notes

Sigma bands are shown as vertical reference lines.

dataviz.qq_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static quantile-quantile plot against a theoretical distribution.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name used as the theoretical reference.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional marker color.

  • reference_color (str) – Reference line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Axes containing the QQ plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or the distribution name is invalid.

Examples

`python ax = qq_plot_static(data, distribution="norm") `

Notes

Points close to the reference line indicate compatibility with the selected distribution.

dataviz.qq_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, distribution: str = 'norm', title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, reference_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 700, width: int = 700, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive quantile-quantile plot against a theoretical distribution.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • distribution (str) – SciPy distribution name used as the theoretical reference.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Optional marker color.

  • reference_color (str) – Reference line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive QQ plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain or the distribution name is invalid.

Examples

`python fig = qq_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

Hover data exposes paired theoretical and sample quantiles.

dataviz.quality_bar(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static bar chart of univariate quality rates.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Bar color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing quality-rate bars.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the input has no observations.

Examples

`python ax = quality_bar_static(series) `

Notes

Rates are shown on the inclusive [0, 1] scale.

dataviz.quality_bar_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive bar chart of univariate quality rates.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • (Optional[str] (color) – Bar color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive quality-rate chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If input data cannot be represented as a pandas Series.

  • ValueError – If the input has no observations.

Examples

`python fig = quality_bar_interactive(series) `

Notes

Quality rates provide a quick screen before selecting downstream visualizations.

dataviz.quantile_bin_plot(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, q: int = 10, statistic: Literal['mean', 'median'] = 'mean', title: str = 'Quantile Bin Plot', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes

Create a static plot of y summaries across x quantile bins.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • q (int) – Number of quantile bins.

  • statistic (Literal["mean", "median"]) – Summary statistic to plot.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Quantile-bin summary plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If quantile bins or statistic are invalid.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.quantile_bin_plot_static(x, y, q=5, statistic="median") `

Notes

Quantile bins keep group sizes roughly balanced.

dataviz.quantile_bin_plot_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, q: int = 10, statistic: Literal['mean', 'median'] = 'mean', title: str = 'Quantile Bin Plot', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Figure[source]

Create an interactive plot of y summaries across x quantile bins.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • q (int) – Number of quantile bins.

  • statistic (Literal["mean", "median"]) – Summary statistic to plot.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Interactive quantile-bin summary plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If quantile bins or statistic are invalid.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.quantile_bin_plot_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

The hover labels use quantile-bin centers on the x-axis.

dataviz.raincloud_plot(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (7, 8), color: str | None = None, alpha: float = 0.6, theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static raincloud-style plot for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Primary plot color.

  • alpha (float) – Point opacity.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing violin, box, and raw points.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If opacity is invalid or no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = raincloud_plot_static(data) `

Notes

The implementation approximates a raincloud with familiar matplotlib primitives.

dataviz.raincloud_plot_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 700, width: int = 700) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive raincloud-style plot for one numeric variable.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Optional y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Primary plot color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive raincloud-style plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = raincloud_plot_interactive(data) `

Notes

Plotly violin traces can display violin, box, meanline, and raw points together.

dataviz.rank_scatter(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, title: str = 'Rank Scatter', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes

Create a static scatter plot comparing ranks of two variables.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Rank scatter axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If fewer than two observations are available.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.rank_scatter_static(x, y) `

Notes

Rank scatter plots emphasize monotonic relationships.

dataviz.rank_scatter_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, title: str = 'Rank Scatter', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Figure[source]

Create an interactive scatter plot comparing ranks of two variables.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Interactive rank scatter plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If fewer than two observations are available.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.rank_scatter_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

Rank scatter plots pair naturally with Spearman correlation.

dataviz.recommended_bin_count(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, method: Literal['auto', 'fd', 'sturges', 'rice', 'sqrt'] = 'auto') → int[source]

Recommend a histogram bin count from common univariate rules.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • method (BinMethod) – Rule name: "auto", "fd", "sturges", "rice", or "sqrt".

Returns:

Recommended positive number of bins.

Return type:

int

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If method is unknown or no observations remain.

Examples

`python bins = recommended_bin_count(data, method="fd") `

Notes

"auto" chooses the larger of Freedman-Diaconis and Sturges recommendations.

dataviz.reference_band_histogram(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, band_color: str = 'gold', mean_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static histogram with mean and standard-deviation reference bands.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • band_color (str) – Reference band color.

  • mean_color (str) – Mean line color.

  • theme (str) – Styling theme name.

Returns:

Axes containing the reference-band histogram.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python ax = reference_band_histogram_static(data) `

Notes

The shaded band spans one sample standard deviation around the mean.

dataviz.reference_band_histogram_interactive(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, band_color: str = 'gold', mean_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive histogram with mean and standard-deviation reference bands.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • bins (int) – Number of histogram bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – Optional x-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Histogram color.

  • band_color (str) – Reference band color.

  • mean_color (str) – Mean line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly template name.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive reference-band histogram.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If no observations remain.

Examples

`python fig = reference_band_histogram_interactive(data) `

Notes

The standard-deviation band is represented as a translucent rectangle.

dataviz.regression_plot(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, degree: int = 1, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, scatter_color: str = 'steelblue', line_color: str = 'crimson', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static scatter plot with a polynomial regression trend line.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • degree (int) – Polynomial degree passed to numpy.polyfit.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • scatter_color (str) – Scatter marker color.

  • line_color (str) – Regression line color.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Regression plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y cannot be converted to numeric arrays.

  • ValueError – If there are too few observations for the requested polynomial degree.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.regression_plot_static(x, y, degree=2) `

Notes

This function intentionally uses NumPy only; no statsmodels dependency is required.

dataviz.regression_plot_interactive(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, degree: int = 1, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, scatter_color: str = 'steelblue', line_color: str = 'crimson', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive scatter plot with a polynomial regression trend line.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • degree (int) – Polynomial degree passed to numpy.polyfit.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • scatter_color (str) – Scatter marker color.

  • line_color (str) – Regression line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to the scatter trace.

Returns:

Interactive regression plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y cannot be converted to numeric arrays.

  • ValueError – If there are too few observations for the requested polynomial degree.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.regression_plot_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

The fitted line is deterministic and uses all provided observations.

dataviz.remove_outliers(data: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, rule: Literal['iqr', 'zscore', 'mad'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 3.0, multiplier: float = 1.5) → OutlierTreatmentResult[source]

Remove observations flagged as outliers.

Parameters:

  • data (SeriesLike) – Input observations.

  • rule (OutlierRule) – Outlier rule: "iqr", "zscore", or "mad".

  • threshold (float) – Threshold for z-score or MAD rules.

  • multiplier (float) – IQR multiplier for Tukey fences.

Returns:

Original values, retained values, and mask.

Return type:

OutlierTreatmentResult

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be converted to numeric data.

  • ValueError – If all observations are removed or rule options are invalid.

Examples

`python result = remove_outliers(values) `

Notes

Removal changes sample size and should be used with care.

dataviz.residual_plot(y_true: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, y_pred: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, edgecolor: str = 'black', linewidth: float = 1.0, marker_size: int = 50, alpha: float = 0.6, line_color: str = 'red', line_style: str = '--', grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static residual diagnostic plot for regression predictions.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • y_true (ArrayLike) – Observed target values.

  • y_pred (ArrayLike) – Predicted target values.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size as (width, height) in inches. Defaults to (10, 6).

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • edgecolor (str) – Configuration value for edgecolor. Defaults to 'black'.

  • linewidth (float) – Configuration value for linewidth. Defaults to 1.0.

  • marker_size (int) – Configuration value for marker_size. Defaults to 50.

  • alpha (float) – Configuration value for alpha. Defaults to 0.6.

  • line_color (str) – Configuration value for line_color. Defaults to 'red'.

  • line_style (str) – Configuration value for line_style. Defaults to '--'.

  • grid (bool) – Configuration value for grid. Defaults to True.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Configuration value for grid_alpha. Defaults to 0.3.

  • theme (str) – Named styling theme applied after the base plot is created. Defaults to 'default'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 10.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 14.

  • label_size (int) – Configuration value for label_size. Defaults to 11.

  • dpi (int) – Configuration value for dpi. Defaults to 100.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context used while building the figure. Defaults to 'default'.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the rendered static chart.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Example

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.residual_plot_static(y_true, y_pred) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.residual_plot_interactive(y_true: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, y_pred: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, title: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, marker_color: str | None = None, marker_size: int = 8, line_color: str = 'red', line_dash: str = 'dash', showlegend: bool = True, hovermode: str = 'closest', template: str = 'plotly', font_size: int = 12, title_size: int = 16, height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive residual diagnostic plot for regression predictions.

Builds the visualization with package defaults while allowing backend-specific customization through keyword arguments where supported.

Parameters:

  • y_true (ArrayLike) – Observed target values.

  • y_pred (ArrayLike) – Predicted target values.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Optional chart title. When omitted, a descriptive title is generated where possible. Defaults to None.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for color. Defaults to None.

  • marker_color (Optional[str]) – Configuration value for marker_color. Defaults to None.

  • marker_size (int) – Configuration value for marker_size. Defaults to 8.

  • line_color (str) – Configuration value for line_color. Defaults to 'red'.

  • line_dash (str) – Configuration value for line_dash. Defaults to 'dash'.

  • showlegend (bool) – Configuration value for showlegend. Defaults to True.

  • hovermode (str) – Configuration value for hovermode. Defaults to 'closest'.

  • template (str) – Plotly template used to style the interactive figure. Defaults to 'plotly'.

  • font_size (int) – Configuration value for font_size. Defaults to 12.

  • title_size (int) – Configuration value for title_size. Defaults to 16.

  • height (int) – Plotly figure height in pixels. Defaults to 600.

  • width (int) – Plotly figure width in pixels. Defaults to 1000.

  • **kwargs (Any) – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to the underlying plotting function.

Returns:

Configured Plotly figure containing the rendered interactive chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If required inputs are not compatible with the plotting backend.

  • ValueError – If input lengths, matrix shapes, or option values are invalid for the requested chart.

Example

`python import dataviz as dv result = dv.residual_plot_interactive(y_true, y_pred) `

Notes

Static functions return matplotlib objects; interactive functions return Plotly figures.

dataviz.residual_relationship(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, degree: int = 1, title: str = 'Residual Relationship', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes

Create a static residual-vs-x plot after polynomial fitting.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • degree (int) – Polynomial degree for fitted values.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Residual plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If degree is invalid for the available observations.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.residual_relationship_static(x, y, degree=2) `

Notes

Patterns in residuals can reveal nonlinearity or heteroscedasticity.

dataviz.residual_relationship_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, degree: int = 1, title: str = 'Residual Relationship', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Figure[source]

Create an interactive residual-vs-x plot after polynomial fitting.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • degree (int) – Polynomial degree for fitted values.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Interactive residual plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If degree is invalid for the available observations.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.residual_relationship_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

The horizontal zero line marks perfect fitted values.

dataviz.resolve_univariate_data(value: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop', require_numeric: bool = False, fallback_name: str = 'Value') → UnivariateInput[source]

Resolve a column name or series-like object for univariate functions.

Parameters:
